# Menopause & midlife health

> Menopause changes a woman's body in real, specific ways — and gets blamed for many
> changes it does not cause. The clinician's job is to tell the two apart, and to know which
> midlife problems a good diet genuinely helps and which it cannot touch.

## Menopause at a glance

Menopause is not a disease; it is a **reproductive transition** — the ovaries wind down, and
**oestrogen falls**. Clinically it is dated to the **final menstrual period (FMP)**: the
years of fluctuation before it are the *perimenopause*, the years after it the
*postmenopause*. In Singapore the mean age of natural menopause is about **49 years**, with
little difference across Chinese, Malay and Indian women [@loh2005sgMenopause].

The reason menopause earns its own chapter is that it reshapes several nutrition-relevant
systems at once — but each through the same two kinds of driver this part of the book returns
to again and again:

- **What changes inside** — the *internal* driver is the fall in oestrogen, which quietly
  rewires bone remodelling, blood lipids and where the body stores fat.
- **What changes outside** — the *external* drivers are the ones a woman (and her clinician)
  can actually pull: physical activity, the food pattern, alcohol, and the flood of "menopause"
  marketing she now meets.

A useful warning up front: the Western script of menopause is **hot flushes**, but that is not
the local picture. In Singaporean surveys the commonest complaints are **aching joints and
muscles and poor sleep**, with classic hot flushes far down the list [@chim2002sgSymptoms;
@logan2023iwhp]. A woman here may never mention a flush — so listen for the aches and the
sleepless nights instead.

## The two midlife myths: weight and "a broken metabolism"

Almost every woman arrives at midlife having been told that menopause will make her fat and
wreck her metabolism. Both beliefs are, in the strict sense, **wrong** — and unpicking them is
the most useful thing this chapter does.

**Myth 1 — "menopause makes you gain weight."** Follow enough women through the transition and
the scale rises **steadily with age**, with *no* jump timed to the FMP: total midlife weight
gain tracks **ageing and lifestyle**, i.e. energy balance, not menopause itself
[@greendale2019swanBodyComp]. What *is* genuinely menopause-linked is **where** the fat sits.
Around the FMP the rate of fat gain roughly doubles and lean (muscle) mass falls, and fat
**redistributes** from the hips to the **abdomen** — the visceral, metabolically active depot
[@greendale2019swanBodyComp]. So the honest message is not "you won't change" but "**the
waistline, not the scale, is the tell**."

**Myth 2 — "my metabolism has crashed."** When total daily energy expenditure is measured
properly and adjusted for how much fat-free mass a person carries, it is **remarkably stable
from about age 20 to 60** — through pregnancy, through menopause — and only begins its slow
decline (~0.7% a year) *after* 60 [@pontzer2021energy]. There is no metabolic cliff at
menopause. Any real fall in the calories a woman burns comes mostly from **losing muscle** —
which is exactly the thing resistance exercise and enough protein can defend.

Put together, the two myths point to one prescription, and it is not aggressive dieting:
**keep the muscle** (protein plus resistance work) and mind the **pattern and the portions**,
because cutting hard only speeds the loss of the lean mass and bone the next sections are
about. Use the timeline below to see why the scale misleads — and toggle the bone and lipid
layers, which the next two sections explain.

<div data-island="menopause-timeline" data-src="/diagrams/menopause-timeline.yml"></div>

## Bone: the one window that closes fast

Oestrogen normally acts as a **brake on bone breakdown**. When it falls, that brake comes off:
bone-resorbing cells become more numerous and longer-lived (the signalling molecule **RANKL**
rises), and resorption outpaces formation [@eghbali2003rankl]. The result is that bone loss,
gradual for most of adult life, **accelerates sharply** in a narrow window — from about a year
before the FMP to two to three years after — reaching roughly **2% a year** and stripping
around **7% of spine bone** across the transition [@greendale2012swanBone]. This matters
locally: Chinese women, Singapore's majority, are among the **faster** losers through this
window [@greendale2012swanBone].

The honest part is what diet can and cannot do here. Adequate **calcium, vitamin D and
protein** are **permissive** — the raw materials remodelling needs — but they do **not** switch
off the oestrogen-driven loss. Routine low-dose calcium-plus-vitamin-D supplements give **no
net fracture-prevention benefit** in community-dwelling women after menopause, and slightly
raise the risk of kidney stones [@uspstfCalcium2018]; save supplements for a genuine shortfall
or diagnosed osteoporosis.

And **protein doesn't "leach" calcium from bone** — the old fear has it backwards: with enough
calcium, *higher* protein tracks with *better* bone and fewer hip fractures
[@groenendijk2019protein]. (The actual protein targets, and the older-adult sarcopenia angle,
sit in the Part I *Macronutrients* and *Healthy Ageing* chapters.)

The strongest lever a woman can actually pull is **mechanical loading**. In one small,
supervised trial, progressive resistance and impact training held or even modestly *raised*
bone density where supplements alone did not [@watson2018liftmor] — a real but bounded effect.
The everyday version is brisk walking or jogging plus some strength work, not a prescription to
self-load heavily.

The osteoporosis diagnosis and drug management belong to the *Bone Health & Osteoporosis*
chapter, and the calcium and vitamin-D reference intakes to the Part I *Micronutrients* chapter;
here the point is only to **recognise the window and act early** — build the bank from food and
loading before it closes.

::: {.callout-tip}
## 🍜 Eating out & 🍳 healthy kitchen: build the bone bank from food first
Local calcium is easier than it looks. Choose **calcium-set tofu and *tau kwa***, order the
**yong tau foo** with more beancurd, keep **ikan bilis** and canned **sardines** (bones and
all) in the kitchen, add **kai-lan and other low-oxalate Asian greens**, and use
**calcium-fortified soy milk**. Pair it with getting some sun and staying active — despite our
climate, about **one in three** mid-life Singaporean women are vitamin-D deficient, from indoor
lives and sun-avoidance rather than lack of sun, with Indian and Malay women at higher risk
[@cheong2021vitDsg]. Local calcium intakes sit at or below target, so food first is a realistic
lever [@hpbNNS2010].
:::

## The cardiometabolic shift: real, but be precise about it

Before menopause, women enjoy a cardiovascular "advantage" over men; the transition quietly
erodes it. The precise, teachable point is that the change is **selective**. **LDL-cholesterol
and apolipoprotein B rise in a sharp step right around the FMP** — that part *is*
menopause-linked — whereas blood pressure, glucose and triglycerides drift up with **age**, not
with the FMP [@matthews2009swanLipids]. Alongside, visceral fat and the incidence of the
metabolic syndrome climb through the transition [@janssen2008swanMetS]. Professional consensus
now treats **midlife as a critical early-prevention window** for women's heart health
[@elkhoudary2020ahaMenopause].

This precision guards against two opposite errors: **over-attribution** ("everything bad at
midlife is menopause" — no, the blood pressure is probably ageing) and **under-attribution**
("nothing has changed" — the cholesterol step and the visceral fat are real). The dietary lever
is the same one the book keeps returning to: a **whole dietary pattern** (Mediterranean- or
DASH-style) rather than chasing single nutrients — taught in the *What a Healthy Diet Looks
Like* chapter, with the actual management of lipids and blood pressure owned by the
*Cardiovascular Disease & Hypertension* chapter. The midlife job is to **start the pattern
early**, not to wait for numbers to go wrong.

## Phytoestrogens and soy: the evidence-versus-hype case

No midlife topic generates more marketing than **soy and "phytoestrogens"** (plant compounds,
chiefly the soy isoflavones, that weakly mimic oestrogen). It is worth getting right because
the popular story is wrong at **both** ends.

**On hot flushes, soy is oversold.** Pooled trials of isoflavone *supplements* show at most a
**modest and inconsistent** reduction in flushes — around a fifth fewer, far weaker and slower
than hormone therapy — and the highest-quality synthesis finds **no reliable benefit** overall,
with a signal only for high-genistein extracts [@taku2012isoflavones; @lethaby2013cochranePhyto;
@chen2015phyto]. Part of the inconsistency is biological: only some people (more in Asian
populations) carry the gut bacteria that convert a soy isoflavone into **equol**, the more
active form [@setchell2002equol], and giving **equol directly** to non-producers helps in
*preliminary* trials [@aso2012sequol]. Menopause societies therefore **do not** recommend soy
or isoflavone supplements as a treatment for flushes [@namsNonhormone2023]. The honest line to a
patient: *soy foods are a fine, healthy part of your diet and reasonable to try, but they are
not hormone therapy and I would not count on them to fix the flushes.*

**On safety, soy is wrongly feared.** Many women have heard that soy "causes breast cancer" or
"feeds" oestrogen-sensitive tumours — a fear that came from **rodent studies that do not
translate to humans**. In people, and especially at the **habitual Asian intakes** relevant
here, soy foods are associated with **lower** breast-cancer risk, and they are **safe for
survivors** — including women with oestrogen-receptor-positive disease and those taking
tamoxifen, who if anything do *better* eating moderate soy [@wu2008soyMeta; @shu2009shanghai;
@nechuta2012abcpp]. The one distinction to keep sharp is **food versus supplement**: the
reassurance is for whole soy foods, not high-dose isoflavone pills, which are a different and
less-tested product.

Running the appraisal toolkit on a claim like this — tracing "soy fixes menopause" or "soy
causes cancer" back to what the studies actually show — is the work of the *Evidence vs Hype &
Everyday Questions* chapter; soy is one of its best worked examples.

::: {.callout-tip}
## 🍜 Eating out: soy is on every hawker menu, and that's a good thing
Singapore's everyday soy — ***tau huay*** and soybean milk, ***tauhu*** and ***tau kwa***,
***tau pok***, ***foo chok*** (beancurd skin), tempeh and edamame — is exactly the food in the
studies that link soy to **lower** breast-cancer risk at local intakes [@wu2008sgSoy]. Enjoy it
as good, protein-rich food; just don't sell it, or buy it, as a flush cure.
:::

## When nutrition is *not* the answer — and other midlife truths

A "midlife literacy" chapter has to be honest about the limits of diet, because that is where
patients get exploited.

- **Iron: needs fall, and new deficiency is a red flag.** Once periods stop, iron requirements
  **drop** (to about the level of an adult man's), so routine iron supplements become
  unnecessary — and the body has no way to offload excess iron [@odsIron]. More importantly,
  **new** iron-deficiency anaemia in a woman past menopause is **not** a life-stage need to
  top up; it points to **blood loss, usually gastrointestinal**, and should be investigated,
  not self-treated.
- **Vaginal and urinary symptoms have no dietary fix.** The genitourinary syndrome of menopause
  (dryness, urinary symptoms) is common locally [@chim2002sgSymptoms] and **no food or
  supplement treats it** — recognise it, and refer for effective therapy (moisturisers, topical
  oestrogen). Be alert to "natural remedy" marketing that targets exactly this symptom.
- **Sleep: use the cheap, real levers.** Poor sleep is one of the top local complaints
  [@logan2023iwhp]. **Limit alcohol** (it fragments sleep), keep **caffeine to earlier in the
  day**, avoid **large late meals**, and mind weight — while remembering that sleep disrupted by
  night sweats or low mood may need referral rather than a dietary tweak.
- **Alcohol: don't prescribe the "wine for the flushes."** Alcohol raises breast-cancer risk
  with **no safe threshold** [@hamajima2002alcohol] and worsens both sleep and bone — so a
  nightly glass is poor midlife advice, never a symptom remedy.
- **Mind the marketing — and do no harm.** There is **no menopause-specific diet pill**;
  obesity medicines belong to the *Obesity & Metabolic Syndrome* chapter, and most "menopause
  supplements" are unproven. And because restrictive dieting and disordered eating are **real**
  in women aged 40–60 [@mangweth2014ed], counsel **weight-inclusively and with consent** —
  anchor on protein, activity and a sound pattern rather than pushing weight loss. The
  *how* of that conversation is the *Behaviour-Change Counselling* chapter's craft.

## The Singapore picture

Singapore is unusually well-studied here, and the local data change how you counsel. Menopause
arrives a touch earlier than the Western "51" (mean **~49 years**) and its face is different:
**aches and broken sleep**, not flushes, dominate, and **Indian women report the heaviest
symptom burden, Chinese the least** [@loh2005sgMenopause; @logan2023iwhp]. Bone risk bites
harder than the sunshine suggests — majority-Chinese bone loss is fast, and vitamin-D
deficiency is common despite the climate [@greendale2012swanBone; @cheong2021vitDsg]. And the
**soy paradox** is native: at the high habitual intakes of Asian diets, soy tracks with *lower*
breast-cancer risk [@wu2008sgSoy]. Finally, menopause is often met with **cultural silence** —
so the clinician frequently has to open the subject gently and normalise it. Singapore now has
its own specialist menopause guidance to refer to when symptoms outrun what nutrition and
lifestyle can do.

## So — is there a "menopause diet"?

No — and promising one does a disservice. Diet and activity do two things extremely well at
midlife: they **protect bone** (food-first minerals plus loading) and they **protect the heart**
(a sound pattern, started early), while a **weight-inclusive** approach avoids the harm of
crash dieting. They do only a **little** for hot flushes, and **nothing** for vaginal dryness.
The skill this chapter builds is precisely that matching — **which midlife problem a good diet
genuinely helps, and which one needs referral instead of a supplement.**

## Check your understanding

<div data-island="quiz" data-src="/quizzes/menopause-midlife.quiz.yml"></div>

## Practise: "can soy replace my hormones?"

<div data-island="case" data-src="/cases/menopause-soy.case.yml"></div>
